title: GDPR Processor Compliance Attestation Tracking

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(3) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(3) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.27: GDPR Processor Compliance Attestation Tracking.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.27' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `gdpr_processor_register.csv` (columns: processor_name, attestation_type, attestation_expiry, dpa_signed), vendor risk platform exports (HEC)
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_processor_register.csv
| eval expiry_epoch=strptime(attestation_expiry,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval days_to_expiry=round((expiry_epoch-now())/86400,0)
| eval attestation_status=case(
    isnull(attestation_expiry), "MISSING",
    days_to_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED",
    days_to_expiry < 60, "EXPIRING",
    1=1, "CURRENT")
| where attestation_status!="CURRENT" OR dpa_signed!="true"
| table processor_name, attestation_type, attestation_expiry, days_to_expiry, attestation_status, dpa_signed
| sort attestation_status, days_to_expiry
description: Article 28 requires processors to demonstrate compliance and controllers to work only with processors offering sufficient guarantees. This use case tracks ISO 27001, SOC 2, and processor-signed security addenda — surfacing expired attestations and processors without current documentation before contract renewal or procurement renews access.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Extend processor inventory with attestation types and expiry dates; (2) integrate procurement / ServiceNow vendor records for automated updates; (3) alert procurement and DPO at 60 and 30 days before expiry; (4) block new data integrations in workflow when `EXPIRED`; (5) export quarterly attestation coverage report. Lookups: `gdpr_processor_register`, `gdpr_processor_register.csv`.
visualization: Table (processors at risk), Single value (expired attestations), Bar chart (status by processor tier), Timeline (expiry dates). Key fields: `attestation_expiry`, `attestation_status`, `attestation_type`, `days_to_expiry`, `dpa_signed`, `expiry_epoch`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `gdpr_processor_register.csv` (columns: processor_name, attestation_type, attestation_expiry, dpa_signed), vendor risk platform exports (HEC).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Extend processor inventory with attestation types and expiry dates; (2) integrate procurement / ServiceNow vendor records for automated updates; (3) alert procurement and DPO at 60 and 30 days before expiry; (4) block new data integrations in workflow when `EXPIRED`; (5) export quarterly attestation coverage report.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_processor_register.csv
| eval expiry_epoch=strptime(attestation_expiry,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval days_to_expiry=round((expiry_epoch-now())/86400,0)
| eval attestation_status=case(
    isnull(attestation_expiry), "MISSING",
    days_to_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED",
    days_to_expiry < 60, "EXPIRING",
    1=1, "CURRENT")
| where attestation_status!="CURRENT" OR dpa_signed!="true"
| table processor_name, attestation_type, attestation_expiry, days_to_expiry, attestation_status, dpa_signed
| sort attestation_status, days_to_expiry
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Processor Compliance Attestation Tracking (Art. 28(3))** — Article 28 requires processors to demonstrate compliance and controllers to work only with processors offering sufficient guarantees. This use case tracks ISO 27001, SOC 2, and processor-signed security addenda — surfacing expired attestations and processors without current documentation before contract renewal or procurement renews access.

Documented **Data sources**: `gdpr_processor_register.csv` (columns: processor_name, attestation_type, attestation_expiry, dpa_signed), vendor risk platform exports (HEC). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **expiry_epoch** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **days_to_expiry** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **attestation_status** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where attestation_status!="CURRENT" OR dpa_signed!="true"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Output fields: attestation_expiry, attestation_status, attestation_type, days_to_expiry, dpa_signed, expiry_epoch, processor_name.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (processors at risk), Single value (expired attestations), Bar chart (status by processor tier), Timeline (expiry dates).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for article 28 requires processors to demonstrate compliance and controllers to work only with processors offering sufficient guarantees. this use case tracks iso 27001, soc 2, and processor-signed sec. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
